content.text: A speech-to-speech (S2S) system takes spoken audio as input and returns spoken audio as output. Two very different architectural families achieve this. The
serialises three independent models — automatic speech recognition (ASR), a large language model (LLM), and text-to-speech synthesis (TTS) — connected by text strings. An

system processes audio tokens directly with no mandatory text bottleneck, which typically halves latency and preserves paralinguistic cues — emotion, accent, speaking rate — that are irreversibly stripped the moment audio becomes a transcript.

Cascade pipelines dominate production because each stage is independently swappable, debuggable, and improvable with domain-specific models. E2E approaches are advancing rapidly but are computationally expensive and available mainly through closed APIs (OpenAI Realtime) or cutting-edge research checkpoints (Moshi, dGSLM).

attrs.alt: Speech-to-Speech architecture comparison: Cascade pipeline (ASR→LLM→TTS, 1.5-4s latency) versus End-to-End approaches (GPT-4o Realtime ~200ms, Moshi ~200ms, dGSLM/Spirit LM) with a 7-row model comparison table

content.text: The cascade (left, amber) passes audio through three serialised models totalling 1.5–4 s. End-to-end systems (right, blue) treat audio tokens as first-class inputs and outputs — the same frame that the model reads is the frame it generates.
Despite its latency disadvantages, the cascade is the practical workhorse of commercial voice agents in 2025. Each stage is a mature, interchangeable component:
— segments the audio stream into utterances, detecting end-of-turn with silence thresholding or a lightweight neural detector (Silero-VAD, WebRTC VAD). Adds ~50 ms of buffering.

— transcribes audio to text. Whisper-large-v3 runs in ~200–400 ms per utterance on a single GPU; streaming models (Deepgram Nova, AssemblyAI Streaming, Whisper.cpp streaming) can emit partial tokens in near-realtime, shrinking perceived latency significantly.

— generates a text response from the transcript. Time-to-first-token (TTFT) dominates: a 7B model on A100 achieves ~150–400 ms; GPT-4o via API is ~300–600 ms. Streaming the response sentence-by-sentence unlocks pipeline overlap with TTS.

— synthesises audio from each sentence as it completes. Fast neural vocoders (XTTS v2, StyleTTS 2, Parler-TTS) add 100–300 ms per sentence; streaming TTS APIs (ElevenLabs, Cartesia Sonic) begin audio playback in under 150 ms from first text input.
A well-optimised streaming cascade reaches 400–800 ms end-to-end — acceptable for asynchronous chatbots but noticeably sluggish compared to the sub-300 ms expectation of telephone conversations.
Why the Text Bottleneck Is a Problem
Transcription is a lossy channel — it discards everything that is not lexical content:
— a frustrated whisper and an excited shout produce identical transcripts. The LLM never sees the original tone, so the TTS re-synthesises a generic neutral voice regardless of user affect.

— natural conversation includes "mm-hmm" and "yeah" while the other party speaks. VAD-based segmentation treats any simultaneous audio as interference; E2E duplex systems process both channels at once.

— ASR errors (homophones, proper nouns, code-switching between languages) corrupt the LLM input. There is no correction signal from downstream stages — the LLM confidently reasons from a flawed premise.
— accent, vocal fingerprint, and language variety are erased. The synthesised voice cannot mirror the caller's accent or adapt to their speaking pace.
E2E models work in audio token space throughout. The critical distinction is
capability: can the system listen and speak simultaneously (like a phone call), or is it
(one party at a time, like a walkie-talkie)? Most cascade systems are half-duplex by design; the best E2E systems are full-duplex.

The Realtime API (launched October 2024) exposes GPT-4o's native audio modality over a WebSocket. The model ingests PCM audio frames and streams PCM audio back in parallel — full-duplex, ~200 ms TTFB, interruption-aware. When the user starts speaking mid-response, a
event fires and the outgoing audio should be cancelled immediately.
Key details: audio is 24 kHz mono PCM16 encoded as base64. The

turn-detection mode means OpenAI's server decides when the user has finished speaking — no client-side VAD required. The API charges per audio-input token and per audio-output token, not per character. A 60-second conversation costs roughly $0.06 in input audio tokens plus output tokens.
Moshi (2024) is the most complete open-source full-duplex S2S model. Its architecture combines three innovations:

— a fully causal streaming neural codec at 12.5 Hz frame rate, 8 codebooks, 1.1 kbps. Comparable quality to EnCodec at 1.5 kbps, with the causal constraint enabling real-time frame-by-frame processing.

— a transformer LM that models two Mimi audio streams simultaneously: the user channel and the Moshi response channel, interleaved frame-by-frame at the token level. The LM sees both streams in the same context window, enabling genuine duplex generation.

— Moshi generates a parallel text stream of internal reasoning alongside each audio frame. This text is invisible to the user but stabilises generation quality: the LM anchors on semantic content before committing to acoustic tokens.

— a smaller secondary transformer that generates all 8 residual codebook tokens for each 80 ms frame, conditioned on the Helium backbone hidden state. This separates coarse semantic decisions (Helium) from fine acoustic detail (depth).

# Install Moshi and start a local inference server
pip install moshi

# Download and serve the 7B checkpoint (requires ~16 GB VRAM)
python -m moshi.server \
    --hf-repo kyutai/moshiko-pytorch-bf16 \
    --port 8998

# Then open the built-in web client at http://localhost:8998
# — or connect from Python using the MoshiClient WebSocket API
These are research milestones rather than deployable products, but they established the conceptual vocabulary used by all subsequent E2E S2S work:

— extends GSLM to two-channel dialogue. HuBERT semantic tokens from each speaker channel feed a dialogue LM implemented as two interleaved GPT-2-style transformers. A HiFi-GAN vocoder reconstructs audio. It can generate realistic turn-taking and backchannels without any text, but is half-duplex and limited to English.

— interleaves text tokens and EnCodec speech tokens in a single transformer vocabulary. You can prompt it with text and receive a speech response, or vice versa. It is the conceptual ancestor of GPT-4o's multimodal token approach but lags it in scale and quality.
S2S pipelines must agree on audio format at every interface. The most common choices:
When building on WebRTC (e.g., browser microphone to Python server), the browser sends Opus-encoded audio. Your server must decode Opus to PCM before passing it to Whisper or the Realtime API.

When the Realtime API is too expensive or unavailable, a well-architected streaming cascade can approach 400–600 ms end-to-end. The key is pipelining: begin TTS as soon as the first sentence exits the LLM while the LLM is still generating the second sentence.

from deepgram import DeepgramClient, LiveOptions, LiveTranscriptionEvents
import asyncio

async def streaming_asr(mic_generator, on_final):
    """Calls on_final(text) for each complete utterance."""
    dg = DeepgramClient(api_key="<DG_API_KEY>")
    opts = LiveOptions(
        model="nova-2",
        language="en-US",
        smart_format=True,
        interim_results=True,    # emit partial transcripts while user speaks
        utterance_end_ms=1000,   # silence window to declare utterance complete
    )
    conn = await dg.listen.asynclive.v("1").start(opts)

    # Fired when a complete utterance arrives (is_final=True, speech_final=True)
    conn.on(LiveTranscriptionEvents.SpeechFinal,
            lambda _, result: asyncio.ensure_future(
                on_final(result.channel.alternatives[0].transcript)
            ))

    async for chunk in mic_generator:
        await conn.send(chunk)

    await conn.finish()

Split LLM output on sentence boundaries, synthesise each sentence as soon as it is complete, and queue audio chunks for immediate playback. This cuts perceived latency to roughly TTFT + TTS-of-first-sentence.

import asyncio, re
from openai import AsyncOpenAI
from cartesia import AsyncCartesia

SENT_RE = re.compile(r'(?<=[.!?])\s+')

async def llm_tts_pipeline(user_text: str, audio_queue: asyncio.Queue):
    oai = AsyncOpenAI()
    tts = AsyncCartesia(api_key="<CARTESIA_KEY>")
    TTS_OPTS = {
        "model_id": "sonic-english",
        "voice": {"mode": "id", "id": "<voice_id>"},
        "output_format": {"container": "raw", "encoding": "pcm_f32le",
                          "sample_rate": 24000},
    }
    buffer = ""

    stream = await oai.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o", stream=True,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_text}],
    )
    async for chunk in stream:
        delta = chunk.choices[0].delta.content or ""
        buffer += delta
        parts = SENT_RE.split(buffer)
        if len(parts) > 1:
            for sentence in parts[:-1]:
                if sentence.strip():
                    # Start TTS immediately — LLM continues in background
                    async for pcm_chunk in tts.tts.sse(transcript=sentence, **TTS_OPTS):
                        await audio_queue.put(pcm_chunk["audio"])
            buffer = parts[-1]

    if buffer.strip():
        async for pcm_chunk in tts.tts.sse(transcript=buffer, **TTS_OPTS):
            await audio_queue.put(pcm_chunk["audio"])
    await audio_queue.put(None)  # sentinel — playback loop stops here
Natural conversation requires the system to yield the floor gracefully and accept interruptions without awkward silence or repeated speech. Three engineering challenges must be solved:

— a simple silence timer ("500 ms quiet → user is done") misfires on pauses within a sentence. Better approaches combine VAD confidence with a lightweight turn-prediction classifier trained on dialogue corpora to distinguish within-turn pauses from genuine handovers.

— when the user speaks while the system is talking, audio playback must stop within 40–80 ms (one to two audio frames) to feel responsive. The cascade needs a cancel signal that flushes the TTS output queue and discards buffered LLM tokens.

— the microphone picks up the system's own speaker output. An AEC running at the audio capture layer (built into WebRTC, available via SpeexDSP on servers) suppresses playback from the input before ASR sees it — otherwise the system will transcribe itself.
Evaluating S2S systems requires both audio quality metrics and conversational effectiveness metrics — no single number captures both dimensions:
Time-to-first-byte: latency from end of user turn to first audio byte out
<300 ms for natural feel; Realtime API ≈200 ms
Real-time factor: generation time ÷ audio duration; <1.0 = faster than real-time
≤0.3 required for live S2S; Moshi ≈0.25 on A100
Mean opinion score: perceived audio quality, 1–5 human raters
Word error rate: ASR accuracy (cascade only)
Whisper large-v3: ~3–5% on LibriSpeech; rises under noise or accent
No universal benchmark; measured on held-out dialogue corpora
Whether system response tone matches user emotion (E2E only)
Measured via SER classifiers; no standard leaderboard yet
The decision in 2025 is primarily driven by latency requirements, cost, and control:

— choose when you need open-source control of every stage, custom domain ASR (medical, legal), a specific LLM not available in E2E form, or per-utterance transcript logging for compliance. Target latency: 400–800 ms.

— choose when sub-300 ms latency is non-negotiable and you accept a closed API with audio-token pricing (~$0.06/min at 2024 pricing). Best for consumer-facing voice experiences where naturalness matters most.

— choose when you need open-source, on-premise full-duplex S2S, are willing to self-host a 7B-parameter model on GPU (≥16 GB VRAM), and the target domain is well-covered by English conversational data.
Cascade pipelines (ASR → LLM → TTS) are the production default but lose prosody, emotion, and speaker identity at the text bottleneck; optimised streaming variants reach 400–800 ms end-to-end.
End-to-end systems (GPT-4o Realtime API, Moshi) process audio tokens natively, enabling ~200 ms TTFB, full-duplex operation, and preservation of paralinguistic cues across the entire exchange.

Moshi (Kyutai, Apache 2.0) uses the Mimi codec (12.5 Hz, 8 codebooks, 1.1 kbps) with a Helium 7B backbone that models user and response audio streams simultaneously frame-by-frame, plus an inner monologue text channel for semantic grounding.

Interruption handling is an engineering requirement, not a feature: the system must detect user speech within 40–80 ms and flush queued audio immediately, requiring acoustic echo cancellation to prevent the ASR from transcribing the system's own voice.

Evaluate S2S systems on TTFB (latency), RTF (realtime capability), MOS (audio quality), WER (cascade ASR accuracy), and turn-detection F1 — no single metric captures the full quality of a conversational voice agent.
